methodology_long: Rankings are generated by a shadow roto model. Each week, all twelve teams are scored across the fourteen active categories (R, HR, RBI, SB, AVG, OBP, SLG, W, K, ERA, WHIP, K/BB, QS, SV+H) based on an estimate of each team's true strength — not by their actual head-to-head matchup result, which depends on schedule luck. Each team receives 12 points for first in a category down to 1 point for last; the Power Score is the sum (max 168, min 14). True strength is estimated with a Bayesian prior (the preseason projection) blended with each completed week's real performance using exponential decay, so recent weeks count more than older ones. Rate categories (ERA, AVG, etc.) are computed from weighted components (e.g., total earned runs over total innings pitched), not from averaging the week-level rates. The Heat Score is the difference between a team's most recent week roto score and its overall power score — positive Heat means the team just had a better week than its season baseline; negative means worse. Final rank order is 85% power, 15% heat.

rankings.blurb: Burns remains the clear top team with a power score of 126, six points clear of the field. The model has them ranked first in AVG, OBP, QS, and K/BB, and top-three in R, W, K, and SV+H. Week 4 was excellent but not outlier-great (47 R, 8 W, 10 QS, 12 SV+H across 81 IP, 3.33 ERA), which is why heat is near zero — Burns is playing at its baseline, and its baseline is the best in the league. The only soft spot is SB (2 all week, last in the league). Otherwise, no obvious weakness to attack.

Caminero jumps three spots to #2 on the back of a dominant Week 4 (48 R, 11 HR, 38 RBI, 6 W, 80 K, 13 SV+H, 4.01 ERA). The model now has them ranked first in W, second in K and ERA and WHIP — the pitching profile is elite across the board. The concerns are SB (9th) and SV+H cumulative (7th despite a big Week 4). The 3.21 K/BB rank (4th) is solid but not top-tier, and the .247 AVG with .342 OBP suggests the offense can still evaporate in any given week. Still, this is a complete team with no single category below 7th.

Still ranked first in R, HR, RBI, and SLG — the offense is historically dangerous. The problem, again, is pitching: last in K and tied for last in W, with the 11th-ranked ERA. Week 4 gave a hint of improvement (10 QS in only 44.1 IP suggests streaming starters hit hard), but the 5.68 ERA that came with it shows the tradeoff. A 1-2-1 actual matchup record despite a top-three power score is partly schedule luck, but it also reflects how lopsided this roster is. If the pitching ever stabilizes, the team becomes the #1. Until it does, they lose three categories every week no matter what.

PO' BOYD has the second-highest raw power score (113, tied with Caminero) but drops a spot because of the -19 heat. Week 4 was a split: the offense erupted (18 HR, 45 RBI, .532 SLG — the best HR and SLG week any team has posted this year) but the pitching was rough (3 W, 4.58 ERA, 1.36 WHIP, 1.76 K/BB). The ugly K/BB rank (10th) is the standout weakness — too many walks relative to the strikeout volume. Still a top-4 team on talent, but the staff has to stop issuing free passes.

The pitching finally showed up in Week 4: 2.80 ERA, 1.00 WHIP, 80 K, 3.81 K/BB. That's the best rate-stat week of any team through four weeks. Combined with 45 R and 9 SB, it was enough to push the model's heat score to +10. The roster is still structurally thin on saves (last in SV+H) and wins (11th), largely because the SP rotation is boom-or-bust (Cole, Glasnow, Snell). Offense ranks 2nd in SB and 4th in R. A couple more weeks like this one and CLAUDE climbs into the top 3.

rankings.team_name: Camp of the New Orleans Saints
rankings.blurb: The hottest team in the league by a wide margin — +28 heat score, the largest in either direction. Week 4 was a category-count explosion: 45 R, 13 HR, 46 RBI (best in league), 5 W, 77 K, 5 QS, 8 SV+H, .255/.338/.479. The first three weeks were so uneven that even this huge spike only moves them to #6, but the trajectory is meaningful. The cumulative cat record (19-32-13) still lags; the model's rank reflects true strength rather than past results. If the offense sustains this level, a top-5 finish in a few weeks is plausible.

Same story every week. The offense ranks 2nd in R, HR, and AVG, and 3rd in OBP — legitimately top-tier. The pitching ranks last or near-last in ERA, WHIP, QS, and K/BB. Week 4 produced 7 W (tied for 2nd most) but at a 5.15 ERA and 1.47 WHIP, which means the team is eating innings without suppressing runs. Until the rotation stabilizes, Y&Y is effectively locked into a five-category-versus-nine-category split every week, and that's not enough to win matchups consistently.

The biggest drop of the week — six spots. The model still ranks Spoons first in both ERA and WHIP, and the pitching in Week 4 held up (3.43 ERA, 1.06 WHIP, 4 W, 65 K). The collapse came entirely on offense: .198 AVG, 7 HR, 27 RBI. Ranked 10th in HR, 10th in RBI, and 11th in AVG overall now. The trade that sends Spoons back up is obvious — they need bats — but on current roster construction this team is lock-step pitching with an offense that can disappear for a full week. The tie with PO' BOYD reflects exactly that dynamic.

The pitching remains the draw: 1st in K, 2nd in QS, 2nd in W. Week 4 continued the trend (4 W, 77 K, 9 QS, 79 IP). But the offense is last or near-last in four categories: AVG (12th), SLG (12th), OBP (9th), RBI (11th), HR (12th). The 13 ties in the cumulative record is the highest in the league, which fits the profile — this roster wins its pitching categories and loses or ties nearly everything on the hitting side. The offense isn't improving; the model still slots them 9th.

Finally out of the cellar on the strength of a solid Week 4 pitching line: 2.83 ERA, 0.99 WHIP, 51 K, 6 QS in 60.1 IP. Still 0-4 in actual matchups, but the process is improving. Offense remains the problem (last in R, last in RBI, 11th in SB), and the 34 R / 21 RBI output in Week 4 isn't going to move that needle. The roster profile — strong bullpen, mid-rotation SPs, thin lineup — suggests a ceiling around 8th unless there's a trade or waiver upgrade to the hitting side.

Week 4 was the best of the year (35 R, 13 HR, 41 RBI, 10 SB, .250/.304/.451) and the heat reflects that (+12). But the structural issues haven't moved: last in K/BB, last in OBP, and 11th in K, QS, and SLG. The starting pitching is essentially 12th — a 5.23 ERA on the week, 2.64 K/BB — and without better SP production the team cannot compete in half the pitching categories. The elite bullpen and SB base is real but only gets you to #11 on its own.

The worst Week 4 of any team: 26 R, 4 HR, .235 AVG, 1 W, 5.78 ERA, 1.48 WHIP, 3 QS, 1 SV+H. Heat is -29, the coldest in the league. The cumulative profile was already weak on the offensive side (11th in R, 11th in HR) and Week 4 exposed the pitching too. The model now ranks Balls last overall. There's no single category where this team is top-4; the highest rankings are K (5th) and W (5th). Without a clear strength, every matchup becomes a coin flip on whichever cats break right, and right now nothing is breaking right.
